content.headline: Fat isn't just calories — it's the vehicle that carries and delivers flavor throughout a dish, and the fat you choose sets the dish's character before anything else goes in.
points: Butter adds nutty richness and browns beautifully — but burns above medium heat, so it needs watching
Olive oil is fruity and grassy with a lower smoke point than neutral oils — use it where its flavor belongs, not for high-heat searing
Fat-soluble flavor compounds in garlic, thyme, and rosemary only bloom fully when cooked in fat, not water
Fat coats the tongue and lingers, giving dishes persistence — too little fat and flavors taste sharp and fleeting
content.tell_me_more: There's a reason restaurant pasta always tastes richer than the same dish made at home with the same ingredients: restaurants mount butter into sauces as a finishing step. A tablespoon of cold butter swirled in at the end doesn't just add richness — it emulsifies with the pasta water and sauce into a glossy coating that clings to each noodle and carries every other flavor with it. This is also why fat-free recipes taste bright at first and hollow afterward: the acid and salt are there, but there's nothing to carry them.

missions.title: Brown Butter Test
missions.description: Make brown butter: melt two tablespoons of butter in a light-colored pan over medium heat and cook, swirling occasionally, until it turns amber and smells nutty. Use it immediately to dress a simple vegetable or drizzle over toast.
missions.why_it_matters: Brown butter is the most dramatic demonstration of fat's flavor transformation — it goes from mild dairy to something that smells like toasted hazelnuts.
missions.prompt: Show the finished brown butter in the pan or just after drizzling — it should be amber to medium-brown in color with small browned milk solids visible at the bottom. Not pale yellow (under-cooked) and not dark brown or black (burned).
reflection_choices: Reached amber with visible brown solids, smelled nutty and rich — stopped it at the right moment
Got there but it was closer to golden than amber — a little underdone but still tasty
Went too far — darker brown and starting to smell bitter rather than nutty
Burned it — went from perfect to black quickly, need to watch more carefully next time

missions.title: Fat Makes It Last
missions.description: Reflect on how the fat in a dish you've cooked recently affected how it tasted — its richness, persistence on the palate, and how the flavors came across.
missions.why_it_matters: Connecting fat to flavor persistence helps you understand why dishes with very little fat often taste bright but hollow.
missions.prompt: Think of a dish you've made that tasted sharp or flat despite having good seasoning. Was there enough fat in it? How long did the flavors linger after a bite? Compare it to a dish with more fat — did the flavors stay with you longer? Describe the difference.
